items.title:
Dear @sama, 

You owe me an apology. You have relentlessly, publicly and privately, attacked my inte...
items.content:
Dear @sama, 

You owe me an apology. You have relentlessly, publicly and privately, attacked my integrity and wisdom since my 2022 paper “Deep Learning is a Hitting a Wall”.  

But in your own way you have just come around to conceding *exactly* what I was arguing in that paper: that current architectures are not enough, and that we need something new, researchwise. beyond a scaling (a “megabreakthough” in your words below).

That’s all I was trying to say.  

And I was right. 

And you should be man enough to admit it. 

Gary

cc @_KarenHao

items.title:
Awesome non-coding Claude Code Loop ideas for business professionals. 

Grab any of these + immediat...
items.content:
Awesome non-coding Claude Code Loop ideas for business professionals. 

Grab any of these + immediately save time:

- Check my email every 15min and ping me if something is related to Project Pluto and needs a decision made
- Every 30min, prep me for my next meeting with attendee context, threads, mtg and crm notes, emails
- Monitor a deal thread every 2h - summarize any new replies related to legal and suggest next moves
- Research competitor announcements every 20min (better than an RSS feed bc you can specify the type of announcement and not keywords)
- Check in on a brand post going viral, summarize the stats and comments
- Watch across all Slack messages for team blockers and flag to me if I should jump in
- Monitor that certain things are working (like your HR tool or something internal you own)
- Watch 10 companies' job boards you want to apply to
- Watch my sent emails and flag if someone on my VIP client list hasn't replied in a way that feels off
- Literally just have it watch everything across all of your tools and proactively flag actions (ex: "hey you should cancel this mtg")

Recruiters with 40 open roles, flag who's going cold. Teachers, flag students falling behind who haven't submitted homework yet. Event planners, monitor weather and vendors day of. Real estate agents, you can watch MLS for new niche listings ("must have at least 2 bay windows and herringbone floors"). Fundraiser, monitor your investor threads and see which ones need intervening. 

Just prompt Claude Code with '/loop <cadence> <task>' like the image example below or feed it this tweet for role-specific ideas

items.title:
Nope, @elonmusk, not even close. Software has not been eaten by AI.

Nobody has replaced an operatin...
items.content:
Nope, @elonmusk, not even close. Software has not been eaten by AI.

Nobody has replaced an operating systems, or Excel or GPS or their favorite browser or favorite AAA video game entirely with AI. The results just aren’t high enough calibre.

Sure, you can use AI for prototyping. And you can use it for simple “CRUD” [Create, Replace, Update and Delete] databases, databases, knockoffs of existing codebases, etc.

But most complex* real world cases, custom software that has been developed over the years is (at least for now) still far, far better than whatever quick prototypes you can get out of AI.

Puts even more pressure on @Tesla_Optimus as it will show off a...
items.content:
This is a World Model breakthrough.

Puts even more pressure on @Tesla_Optimus as it will show off a new humanoid in April. Version 3.0.

Next week at @nvidia GTC the bar goes even higher, I hear.

My money is that Optimus takes all the oxygen away from the others for months.

I have been pitched thousands of companies in my life, including Siri, Insta360, and Matic Robots in my home. Each a pioneer in using AI in consumer product.

Only Elon broke three laws when he demonstrated his to me.

And I loved every minute of it.

He doesn’t have to break laws anymore, but what if Optimus drove a new Roadster to the event through Austin traffic?

Which robot is driving?

Anyway even if Elon fumbled the robot the robots are still winning.

And I expect Optimus will live up to the hype that it is the biggest launch of all time.

I should have put a bet in on one of those prediction markets before writing this. But didn’t.

And I loved every minute of it.

He doesn’t have to break laws anymore, but what if Optimus drove a new Roadster to the event through Austin traffic?

Which robot is driving?

Anyway even if Elon fumbled the robot the robots are still winning.

And I expect Optimus will live up to the hype that it is the biggest launch of all time.

I should have put a bet in on one of those prediction markets before writing this. But didn’t.

items.title:
GLM-5-Turbo just made long-running AI agents dramatically faster.

https://t.co/Scz5EoTyEi released ...
items.content:
GLM-5-Turbo just made long-running AI agents dramatically faster.

https://t.co/Scz5EoTyEi released a speed-optimized variant of GLM-5, built specifically for multi-step agentic workflows.

> #1 open-source on agentic tasks
> 77.8% on SWE-bench (real coding tasks)
> 92.7% on AIME 2026 (hard math)
> Closes the gap with Claude Opus 4.5

The base model is a 744B MoE architecture with only 40B active parameters at inference time. It uses DeepSeek Sparse Attention to cut deployment costs while keeping a 200K context window intact.

The Turbo variant pushes this further for speed.

OpenClaw is an agent framework that runs multi-step tasks across tools, files, and terminals. Before this, fast and capable was a tradeoff.

Now you can plug a frontier-level model directly into long-horizon agent pipelines without sacrificing response speed. It ships on OpenRouter and via API today, closed-source for now, with all findings going into the next open-source release.

https://t.co/0bdaKBV9E2

items.title:
A team from Stanford and Princeton released LabClaw.

It is a library of AI skills for biomedical ag...
items.content:
A team from Stanford and Princeton released LabClaw.

It is a library of AI skills for biomedical agents built on OpenClaw. 

Each skill tells an agent three things:
1. When to use a tool
2. How to call it
3. What output to produce 

It covers seven research domains:
> 66 biology skills: genomics, proteomics, single-cell analysis
> 36 drug discovery skills: docking, molecular ML, cheminformatics
> 29 literature skills: search, citations, patents, grants
> 20 clinical skills: trials, oncology, medical imaging
> 48 general skills: statistics, ML, data visualization
 
Before this, connecting genomics pipelines, lab robots, and literature search inside one agent meant building everything yourself. 
 
Now a single install command gives your agent pre-wired workflows for single-cell omics, oncology research, and academic search.

@mattpocockuk @theo I'm grading ourselves but I would model this as closest.

We always strive to do...
items.content:
@mattpocockuk @theo I'm grading ourselves but I would model this as closest.

We always strive to do right by all our users, but we've seen incredible growth since January and as you both know it is very difficult to scale a team fast enough to match growth. 

We have users using AI for the first time on ClaudeAI, people learning to code using Claude Code, enterprises switching legacy workflows and power users using the Agent SDK amongst many others.

People at Anthropic are often across many of these user archetypes at once and there has been a lot of challenges dealing with the especially crazy recent growth.

We have not done fully right by Agent SDK users I agree, but it is not for lack of trying or wanting to and I cannot fault anyone at Anthropic for it.

That said, I always admire that @theo holds us to high expectations and I agree that we should meet them. We can always be better.

items.title: I hope you are enjoying my #PiDay #PiDay26 #PiDay2026 posts today. As we approach the end, here are ...
items.content:
I hope you are enjoying my #PiDay #PiDay26 #PiDay2026 posts today. As we approach the end, here are a few more interesting π facts:

1) π is an irrational, transcendental number that never ends or repeats, yet it appears frequently in natural phenomena and engineering.

2) If you randomly select two integers, the probability that they are coprime (share no common factors other than 1) is 6/(π^2).

3) At the 762nd decimal place of π, there is a sequence of six 9s in a row (a one-in-a-million occurrence).

4)  While we know trillions of digits of π, NASA needs only the first 15 or 16 decimal places to calculate interplanetary navigation with very high precision.

5) Fun classroom exercise: Estimate π using random points (jelly beans, darts, random number generator, etc.)! Place a unit circle (radius r=1) inside a 2x2 square. Generate random points within the square and calculate the ratio of points falling inside the circle to the total points. π is approximately 4 times (points in circle/total points). Why? Because the ratio of points in the circle to total points is asymptotically equal to the ratio of the areas of circle to square, which is π/4. [The more points, the higher the accuracy of the estimate.]

items.title: Y2K was child’s play. Most people don’t realize this happened — the day after September 2, 1752 in t...
items.content:
Y2K was child’s play. Most people don’t realize this happened — the day after September 2, 1752 in the USA was September 14, 1752 — as a result of the conversion from the Julian to the Gregorian calendar. Similar jumps in the calendar occurred in other countries, though on different dates. Learn more: https://t.co/O7EKlE2vXf

This loss of ~1/3 of a month generated quite an outcry from monthly renters against some landlords who expected a full month’s rent.

items.title: I can tell you how it’s going to work out: it will fail. I deliberately moved to a *very* walkable n...
items.content: I can tell you how it’s going to work out: it will fail. I deliberately moved to a *very* walkable neighborhood in Florida, with shops, restaurants, gyms, etc. within a super easy reach. People still *completely* stay to themselves and hardly ever interact with each other. Aside from a dog walkers and a few dedicated retirees you never see anyone outdoors, even on a perfect weather Sunday afternoons. Generations’ worth of ingrained cultural habits are hard to break.

items.title:
I think I agree with you kind of

But I think citizenship should still be possible to get after 10 y...
items.content:
I think I agree with you kind of

But I think citizenship should still be possible to get after 10 years of cultural assimilation, learning the language, large financial tax and spending contributions, real work, absolutely zero crime (okay a speeding ticket is fine but not much else), like I'm a positive immigrant in Portugal and should get rewarded for that

More than that though I think welfare should ONLY be for citizens not for residents

It should be practically impossible to get free government money as a non-citizen, that includes unemployment, social housing, income support (free money), and even healthcare: healthcare can be insured privately for non-citizens fine with insurance companies, there's no reason the government should be paying for non-citizens

I remember during COVID, entrance to many countries as a tourist required getting private healthcare, I got that to enter Thailand in 2021

All these welfare programs when they started in the 1960s were meant for citizens, not residents

You'll see how unattractive Europe becomes for welfare seekers overnight when you do this

And you'll be left with people coming to Europe who actually want to contribute!

The GOOD immigrants!

items.title:
What does success look like 12 months in if you go all in on a frictionless Commerce Chain?

I asked...
items.content:
What does success look like 12 months in if you go all in on a frictionless Commerce Chain?

I asked Lisa Martin, CRO of #Conga at #CongaConnect. Her answer was precise.

Deal velocity accelerates. Deal volume grows. Revenue leakage stops hiding in quarterly post-mortems.

Most organizations look at discounting retrospectively. A scattergram tells you where you bled. Commerce Chain stops the bleeding.

Faster deals. Protected margin. Smarter renewals.
That is the future of the revenue chain.

👉 Read #CXOSpice newsletter: https://t.co/33GoZZlXqM

items.title: I know 100% that people will argue with me over this, but I miss when movies were professionally lit...
items.content: I know 100% that people will argue with me over this, but I miss when movies were professionally lit, when actors were intentionally blocked, and when more than teal, orange and beige were allowed to be on the screen. The medium has something to do with it--film made a lot of these things fundamentally necessary--but I think it's more complex than just that. The last few years' movies are just not pleasant to look at, with very few exceptions, and the change occurred sometime around 2015.
items.author: @Gargron@mastodon.social
